title: Libra
author: Don DeLillo
description_or_excerpt: A fictionalized account of Lee Harvey Oswald and the Kennedy assassination, structured around conspiracy, historical reconstruction, and the difficulty of locating agency inside political systems.
ontological_justification: The world remains historically grounded even though causality becomes suspicious and distributed.
affective_justification: The central atmosphere is systemic paranoia, where every fact seems absorbed by conspiracy.
media_justification: The form is dense and historically layered but not primarily typographic or materially disruptive.
reliability_warning: Based on summary rather than full novel; canonical reputation may inflate paranoia score.
student_notes: Good example of high paranoia but low ontology fracture.

title: House of Leaves
author: Mark Z. Danielewski
description_or_excerpt: A layered narrative about a house whose interior dimensions exceed its exterior, presented through nested documents, footnotes, typography, editorial apparatus, and fragmented reading paths.
ontological_justification: The physical space violates stable reality rules and the narrative frame multiplies uncertain levels of mediation.
affective_justification: The text produces claustrophobic dread through both haunted space and unstable documentation.
media_justification: The book's typography, footnotes, and visual layout are central to the reading experience.
reliability_warning: Summary emphasizes famous formal traits; full reading would be needed to test the score.
student_notes: Strong example of high ontology and high media friction.

title: Gravity's Rainbow
author: Thomas Pynchon
description_or_excerpt: A dense postwar novel connecting rockets, sexuality, paranoia, science, bureaucracy, and historical systems through sprawling episodes and shifting styles.
ontological_justification: Reality is not simply broken, but causality and historical structure become unstable and networked.
affective_justification: The mood is dominated by systemic dread, surveillance, and the sense of invisible structures controlling life.
media_justification: The reading friction comes from density, discontinuity, style shifts, and encyclopedic structure rather than visual typography alone.
reliability_warning: Summary cannot capture sentence-level difficulty or full stylistic range.
student_notes: Useful for discussing whether density equals media friction.

title: Infinite Jest
author: David Foster Wallace
description_or_excerpt: A sprawling novel about entertainment, addiction, tennis, recovery, and media saturation, famous for extensive endnotes and a fragmented structure.
ontological_justification: The world is exaggerated and near-future but not fully ontologically unstable.
affective_justification: Addiction, compulsion, entertainment, and exhaustion dominate the affective field.
media_justification: The endnote system and scale create major cognitive and navigational friction.
reliability_warning: The description highlights form more than local textual evidence.
student_notes: Good case for media friction without total reality collapse.

title: Slaughterhouse-Five
author: Kurt Vonnegut
description_or_excerpt: A nonlinear antiwar novel in which Billy Pilgrim becomes unstuck in time, moving between wartime trauma, domestic life, and alien temporality.
ontological_justification: The protagonist's experience of time is structurally fractured and central to the book's ontology.
affective_justification: The affect is traumatic detachment mixed with irony and fatalistic calm.
media_justification: The form is nonlinear but comparatively readable and not materially difficult.
reliability_warning: A short summary may overstate the simplicity of the form.
student_notes: Good example for discussing trauma and temporal ontology.
